coredata.dc:description: Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), a promising liquid fuel and environmentally friendly oxidant, can be produced efficiently through photocatalysis, one of the most green and effective methods. Herein, we demonstrate the use of carbon dots (CDs) modified nickel (II) graphdiyne (CDs/Ni-GDY) as a photocatalyst to achieve efficient production of H2O2 under visible light irradiation without sacrificial agents. The optimum CDs/Ni-GDY photocatalyst exhibits high efficiency for photocatalytic H2O2 production, with a production rate of 1755.18 μmol·h−1·g−1 under visible-light irradiation (λ ≥ 420 nm) in pure water, through simultaneously occurring two-electron oxygen reduction reaction and four-electron water oxidation reaction. In this system, the nickel atoms serve as the active sites, while CDs enhance light absorption and O2 adsorption and increase the interfacial electron transfer process. This work elucidates the structure–activity relationship between the active sites and the promoter of CDs, achieving an improvement in their efficient catalytic performance and providing a new perspective for photocatalytic material design.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots modified metallated graphdiyne with enhanced electron transfer and O2 adsorption capacity for efficient H2O2 photoproduction

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: CDs modified nickel (II) graphdiyne (CDs/Ni-GDY) was used as a photocatalyst to achieve efficient production of H2O2 without sacrificial agents with a production rate of 1755.18 μmol·h−1·g−1 under visible-light irradiation (λ ≥ 420 nm) in pure water, through simultaneously occurring two-electron oxygen reduction reaction and four-electron water oxidation reaction.

Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), a promising liquid fuel and environmentally friendly oxidant, can be produced efficiently through photocatalysis, one of the most green and effective methods. Herein, we demonstrate the use of carbon dots (CDs) modified nickel (II) graphdiyne (CDs/Ni-GDY) as a photocatalyst to achieve efficient production of H2O2 under visible light irradiation without sacrificial agents. The optimum CDs/Ni-GDY photocatalyst exhibits high efficiency for photocatalytic H2O2 production, with a production rate of 1755.18 μmol·h−1·g−1 under visible-light irradiation (λ ≥ 420 nm) in pure water, through simultaneously occurring two-electron oxygen reduction reaction and four-electron water oxidation reaction. In this system, the nickel atoms serve as the active sites, while CDs enhance light absorption and O2 adsorption and increase the interfacial electron transfer process. This work elucidates the structure–activity relationship between the active sites and the promoter of CDs, achieving an improvement in their efficient catalytic performance and providing a new perspective for photocatalytic material design.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is extensively used in bleaching, oxidation, disinfection and other fields [1–4]. The photocatalytic method for generating H2O2 uses water (H2O) and oxygen (O2) as raw materials, with sunlight as the energy source, avoiding the use of flammable and explosive H2 gas and organic solvents in traditional anthraquinone method, and reducing environmental pollution [5]. Despite the significant advantages of photocatalytic H2O2 production such as green environmental protection, low energy consumption, high safety, and strong scalability, it also faces challenges such as limited light absorption capacity, rapid recombination of charge carriers, insufficient surface active sites, and the need to improve stability [6].

Graphdiyne (GDY) is considered to be the most stable form of diacetylenic carbon allotropes [7], in which the benzene rings are connected by butadiyne linkages (−C≡C–C≡C–). Notably, metal elements can be incorporated into the GDY framework as a novel form of molecular functionality, leading to the creation of metallated graphdiyne [8,9]. Recently, the metallated graphdiyne-based nanomaterials have shown great potential in photocatalytic applications, such as photocatalytic H2 production, CO2 reduction, and N2 reduction reactions [10–12]. However, research on their use for photocatalytic H2O2 generation remains limited. On the other hand, carbon dots (CDs) are widely used in the enhancement of photoelectrocatalytic performance due to their tunable fluorescence properties, ease of surface modification and diverse photoelectrochemical properties [13–23]. The combination of CDs with metallated graphdiyne enables the development of efficient photocatalysts for photocatalytic H2O2 generation.

Herein, we demonstrate that CDs modified nickel (II) graphdiyne (CDs/Ni-GDY) can be used as a photocatalyst to achieve efficient production of H2O2 under visible light irradiation without sacrificial agents for the first time. The optimal CDs/Ni-GDY photocatalyst exhibits high efficiency for photocatalytic H2O2 production under visible-light irradiation (λ ≥ 420 nm) in pure water, with a production rate of 1755.18 μmol·h−1·g−1. Based on experimental testing and theoretical calculations, nickel atoms in Ni-GDY are the active centers, while CDs serve as the promoter in the composite photocatalyst. Transient photovoltage (TPV) and transient photocurrent scanning (TPS) tests were employed to analyze reaction dynamics, O2 adsorption, and other photocatalytic characteristics. The photocatalytic tests and in-situ study jointly suggest that the addition of CDs not only enhances the adsorption capacity of the catalyst for O2 but also increases the ability of electron transfer in the composite system, thereby achieving efficient generation of H2O2 through simultaneous two-electron oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) and four-electron water oxidation reaction (WOR) pathways.

8.0 mL of absolute ethanol (C. EtOH) and 4 mmol of NiCl2·6H2O were mixed in a two-neck round-bottom flask under stirring. The solution was deoxygenated by N2 purging for 20 min. Under a N2 atmosphere, 8.0 mL of triethylphosphine (PEt3) was added dropwise, inducing a color change from bright green to deep red. The mixture was heated to 80°C for 1 h, then cooled to room temperature. An ice bath was used to accelerate cooling, and stirring was continued for an additional hour to facilitate recrystallization.

A mixture of 1.5 mmol NiCl2 (PEt3)2, 0.5 mmol GDY (3:1 M ratio), and 0.003 mmol CuI was dissolved in 10 mL Et3N and 10 mL THF in a Schlenk tube. The solution in the Schlenk tube was subjected to oxygen removal using the freeze–pump–thaw method, which involved three cycles of freezing in liquid nitrogen, evacuating under vacuum, and thawing at room temperature. Afterwards, the Schlenk tube was heated sequentially at 30 °C (24 h), 60 °C (48 h), and 80 °C (24 h) under N2 atmosphere. The product was purified by Soxhlet extraction using CH3OH, CH3CN, and THF, yielding 76.5 % Ni-GDY.

The CDs with the size of approximately 5 nm were synthesized following the procedures outlined in our group's previous work [21,24–26]. Ni-GDY and CDs were mixed and ground. The mixture was then transferred into a Schlenk tube, and heated at 60 °C in N2 atmosphere for 24 h to obtain the samples. The obtained samples were designated as x% CDs/Ni-GDY, where x% (5, 10, 15, 20, 25 %) represents the weight ratio of the initially added CDs to Ni-GDY.

In this work, different weight ratio (5 %, 10 %, 15 %, 20 % and 25 %) of CDs in CDs/Ni-GDY were synthesized and tested their photocatalytic performance. The 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY was taken as example to characterize its structure, morphology and basic properties. Fig. S4 displays the scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY and Ni-GDY, illustrating the morphology as micron-sized rod-like clusters with lengths ranging from 10 to 35 μm. Fig. 1 a and b show the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY. As shown, the lattice spacings of 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY with the 0.276 nm and 0.210 nm were measured, consistenting respectively to the (0 2 0) crystal plane of Ni-GDY and the (100) crystal plane of CDs, which demonstrate the CDs were incorporated onto Ni-GDY. The High-Angle Annular Dark Field-Scanning Transmission Electron Microscope (HAADF-STEM) and corresponding elemental mapping images for 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY were taken shown in Fig. 1c–f, demonstrating the uniform distribution of carbon (C), nickel (Ni) and phosphorus (P) elements in 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY. Further, to determine the detailed structure of Ni-GDY, a series of measurements for the structural characterization on Ni-GDY were conducted, including the fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR),

13C nuclear magnetic resonance (13C NMR), and powder X-ray diffraction (XRD). FTIR spectra of CDs, GDY, Ni-GDY and 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY are exhibited in Fig. S5. As shown by the orange line (GDY), the peaks located at 2110 and 3278 cm−1 should attribute to the stretching vibration peaks of carbon hydrogen bonds on alkynes. For CDs (wathet blue trace in Fig. S5), the peak at 3417 cm−1 corresponds to the O-H bond stretching vibration. Additionally, the peaks at 1714 cm−1 and 1587 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibrations of C=O and C-C bonds, respectively. The disappearance of the − C≡CH bond and the appearance of characteristic signals of − C≡C− (2166 cm−1) and –CH2CH3 (2877 cm−1 and 2929 cm−1) from Ni-GDY (dark blue trace in Fig. S5) verify the successful formation of − C≡C − Ni(PEt3)2 − C≡C − fragment in the structures. The weak signal of − C≡CH in the Ni-GDY and 10 %CDs/Ni-GDY is assigned to the few uncondensed acetylene groups at the termini. Fig. S6 reveals the structural formula of Ni-GDY, where the M represents the structure of metal Ni.

Fig. 1g presents the 13C NMR spectrum of Ni-GDY, revealing five distinct signals. The peak at 202.88 ppm corresponds to the −C≡Ni- structure, while the peak at 106.86 ppm is associated with carbon in aromatic rings. The chemical shift at δ = 60.72 ppm corresponds to carbon in alkynyl groups, and the shifts at δ = 10.59 and δ = 8.08 ppm are attributed to saturated carbon atoms within the triethyl-phosphine (PEt3) groups. Based on the comprehensive characterization of the chemical structure of Ni-GDY, the detailed molecular structure well matched the schematic diagram in Fig. S6.

The XRD pattern of Ni-GDY shown in Fig. 1h (dark blue trace) reveals the peaks at 7.7°, 8.3°, 11.1°, and 14.3° corresponding to the (100), (002), (012) and (020) facets, respectively. The matching XRD pattern of 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY verifys that the addition of CDs did not alter the structure of Ni-GDY. In addition, due to the amount of CDs and the same structure of carbon, the XRD pattern of CDs did not show any new diffraction peaks in Fig. 1h.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was provided to further ascertain the elemental composition and chemical bonding in the 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY. Fig. S7 indicates the full survey XPS spectra of GDY, Ni-GDY and 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY, where the C and O elements reveal in the spectrum of GDY, and the Ni, C, O and P elements show in the spectrum of Ni-GDY, while the Ni, C, O and P elements also present in the spectrum of 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY (The signal of the Si peak comes from the substrate being tested.) Fig. S8 displays the high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s for the three samples. The C 1 s spectrum of GDY exhibits three peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.2 eV, and 288.6 eV, which are assigned to the C–C, C–O, and C=O bonds, respectively [27]. However, the C 1 s spectra of Ni-GDY and 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY display two new peaks at 282.8 (282.7) eV and 280.4 (280.1) eV, potentially associating with the C-P bond and C-Ni bond (Fig. S8) [28,29]. The deconvoluted P 2p spectrum in Fig. 2 a distinctly shows two peaks at 132.3 (132.5) and 130.7 eV, corresponding to the P 2p1/2 and P 2p3/2 species in P-Ni bonds. The peak at 134.1 (134.0) eV can be attributed to the binding energies of P-C [30]. The Ni 2p XPS spectra of Ni-GDY and 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY in Fig. 2b exhibit four peaks. The peak at 855.5 eV can contribute to Ni2+, the peak around 873.2 (872.9) belong to Ni-P, and the peaks at 861.1 (861,0) eV and 878.7 (878.8) eV are satellite peaks [31,32]. The atomic percentage of Ni was calculated to be 3.35 % based on XPS results (Table S1). These results indicate that Ni element in 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY exists with Ni2+ coordination form, with Ni-C and Ni-P bonds presented within the 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY composites. Moreover, Raman spectra of the Ni-GDY and GDY in Fig. S9 display two peaks at 1369 cm−1 and 1548 cm−1, assigning to the D and G peaks, respectively.

To gain deeper insights into the local chemical and structural environment, X-ray absorption near-edge structure (XANES) at the Ni K-edge for Ni-GDY were employed. Based on the XANES spectra (Fig. 2c, d), the valence state of Ni in the sample lies between 0 and + 2, approaching + 2. The corresponding EXAFS patterns (Fig. 2e) align well with the expected structure. From the R-fitting plot, a distinct Ni–C/P peak is observed at approximately 1.6 Å, with both Ni-C and Ni-P coordination numbers close to two. Additionally, the wavelet transformation (WT) patterns (Fig. 2f) confirm that the sample primarily consists of Ni–C/P structures.

The light absorption properties and band structures of the photocatalysts were analyzed using UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–Vis DRS). As shown in Fig. 3 a, all of the 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY, Ni-GDY and GDY exhibit broad-spectrum light absorption capabilities, with absorption edges extending from 250 to 800 nm. Compared to GDY, the absorption spectrum of Ni-GDY shows a similar visible-light response (300–800 nm), suggesting that the introduction of nickel does not significantly change the light absorption capabilities. Furthermore, the addition of CDs with Ni-GDY significantly enhances its light absorption capacity. The band gap energy (Eg) of the samples was estimated using the Logarithmic derivative method, as illustrated in Fig. 3b [33,34]. The Eg value of Ni-GDY was calculated to be 1.68 eV.

The Mott-Schottky test was conducted to investigate the conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) positions of Ni-GDY. The Mott-Schottky plots with different frequencies of Ni-GDY display positive values, as shown in Fig. S10, indicating n-type semiconductor characteristics. The flat band potential (Efb) was determined using these plots and corresponds to the Fermi level. The conduction band position (ECB) of n-type semiconductors was approximately 0.1 to 0.2 V more negative than the flat-band potential (Efb) [35]. The derived Efb of Ni-GDY is −0.195 V vs. reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE). Typically, the conduction band (ECB) position of Ni-GDY is −0.295 V vs. RHE [34,36]. The valence band (EVB) potential of Ni-GDY is determined to be 1.385 V vs. RHE. In Fig. 3c, the CV curve of Ni-GDY illustrates the CB and VB positions to be −0.289 V and 1.408 V, respectively (vs. RHE). The band gap (Eg) of Ni-GDY was determined to be 1.697 eV using the formula Eg = EVB − ECB. The CV test results are consistent with those obtained in previous experiments. The CV test results for GDY and 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY are presented in Fig. S11. The CB and VB positions of GDY are −0.292 V and 1.448 V, respectively, while those of 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY are −0.329 V and 1.546 V. These results indicate that the introduction of CDs slightly altered the CB and VB positions of Ni-GDY, lowering the CB and raising the VB. Based on the CV tests, the band structure diagrams for GDY and Ni-GDY are shown in Fig. 3d. The ECB level of both GDY and Ni-GDY lies above the reduction potential for O2/H2O2, while the EVB is below the oxidation potential for H2O/O2, indicating that the bandgap structure of these materials is suitable for photocatalytic H2O2 production.

To probe the internal resistance of charge transfer (Rct), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was utilized. As depicted in Fig. S12, Ni-GDY demonstrates a diminished Rct in comparison with that of GDY, indicating that the incorporation of Ni facilitates charge transfer [37,38]. Additionally, 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY shows a smaller arc radius than that of Ni-GDY, suggesting reduced charge transfer resistance and an increased interfacial charge transfer rate [36,39]. Fig. S13 displays the transient photocurrent response (TPR) curves of CDs, Ni-GDY and 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY, elucidating their respective photoelectrochemical properties. 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY composite manifests a superior photocurrent in contrast to the pristine Ni-GDY, suggesting enhanced electron-hole separation efficiency in 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY [38,40].

The photocatalytic activity of CDs, pristine GDY, Ni-GDY and x% CDs/Ni-GDY (x = 5, 10, 15, 20, 25) were investigated under visible light irradiation without sacrificial agents. The concentration of H2O2 produced was quantified using the titanium sulfate method. As shown in Fig. S15, the GDY and CDs yielded only marginal amount of H2O2 in the system (38.65 and 391.65 μmol·h−1·g−1, respectively), suggesting a limited capacity for H2O2 generation from water under visible light irradiation. Fig. 4 a exhibits the H2O2 yields with Ni-GDY and x% CDs/Ni-GDY as photocatalysts. By comparison, Ni-GDY as photocatalyst could produce the H2O2 at an average rate of 1525.81 μmol·h−1·g−1, representing a significant enhancement in the catalytic activity of Ni modified GDY. Moreover, the catalytic activity of the x% CDs/Ni-GDY catalyst obviously increased after the addition of CDs, while 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY achieved a superiors H2O2 production rate of 1755.18 μmol·h−1·g−1. However, excess addition of CDs (after 10 %, from 15 to 25 %), the photocatalysts inhabit the H2O2 generation, suggesting excessive CDs will cover the active sites of the photocatalyst, leading to catalytic degradation. Fig. S16 illustrates that neither Ni-GDY nor GDY exhibits H2 production when utilized as photocatalysts. Time-dependent curves depicting H2O2 production catalyzed by GDY, Ni-GDY and 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY are presented in Fig. 4b. Under dark conditions, no H2O2 was detected. Following 24 h, as the H2O2 concentration yield stabilizes, it indicates that the reaction has reached equilibrium [41]. Compared to other photocatalysts for H2O2 production in Table S2, 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY exhibits superior photocatalytic activity.

To evaluate the reusability of 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY, a cyclic stability experiment was conducted. Fig. 4c demonstrates that 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY maintains stable and efficient catalytic performance over five cycles. Fig. S17 exhibits the repetitive performance of Ni-GDY and GDY. The TEM image of 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY after five cycles (Fig. S18) reveals negligible morphological change compared to its pre-reaction state, indicating excellent morphological stability. Additionally, characteristic peaks observed in the XRD pattern and FTIR spectrum of 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY after the reaction (Figs. S19 and S20) confirm its structural stability. Moreover, the XPS spectrum of Ni-GDY (Fig. S21) shows no differences compared to those obtained before the reaction, indicating unchanged chemical composition. Consequently, 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY demonstrates favorable morphology and structural stability after the catalytic reaction. Apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) serves as a crucial indicator for evaluating the light utilization efficiency of photocatalysts [42]. Fig. 4d illustrates the AQE with various wavelengths of 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY corresponded with the UV–vis DRS of 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY. At incident wavelengths of 365 nm and 420 nm, the AQE values were determined to be 1.57 % and 1.70 %, respectively. The experimental results demonstrate that the incorporation of CDs significantly enhanced the AQE of the 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY composite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reaction pathway and mechanism of photocatalytic H2O2 production
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the reaction pathway of H2O2 production catalyzed by 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY, the H2O2 yields of photocatalyst (5 mg) over 4 h in different atmospheres (N2/O2) were investigated (Fig. 5 a). Compared to H2O2 production under air conditions, the yield significantly decreased in the N2 atmosphere and increased in the O2 atmosphere, demonstrating the critical role of O2 in the catalytic process. To further investigate the reaction pathway of H2O2 generation, the active species trapping experiments were conducted to validate the reaction pathway (Fig. 5b) [43]. AgNO3 and EDTA-2Na were used as electron (e-) and hole (h+) scavengers, respectively. As depicted in Fig. 5b, the rate of H2O2 production decreased significantly when electrons were captured, whereas it increased notably upon hole capture. These results suggest that H2O2 generation is predominantly governed by the ORR, and trapping of holes facilitates greater electron involvement in ORR. Consequently, H2O2 formation occurs via the ORR pathway.

To elucidate the reaction mechanism underlying H2O2 generation by the catalyst, rotating ring-disk electrode (RRDE) chronoamperometric curves were employed under varying experimental conditions to analyze electron transfer dynamics [44]. The potential of the Pt ring electrode was maintained at 0.9 V to detect the H2O2 generated on the disk electrode. Fig. 5c illustrates the i-t curve obtained using an RRDE as the working electrode and an Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode under O2 saturated 0.1 M TBAP anhydrous acetonitrile solution, transitioning from dark to light conditions. The increase in current following light exposure in Fig. 5c (red line) indicates the capability of 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY for oxygen reduction. Simultaneously, an increase in current on the Pt ring electrode following light exposure (blue line) suggests the reduction of O2 to H2O2 by 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY. The ORR electron transfer number (n) of Ni-GDY was calculated from Fig. 5c to be 2.13, indicating that the photoinduced water decomposition reaction catalysed by 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY follows a two-electron pathway (S2 Part, Supporting Information, provides detailed calculations). Fig. 5e exhibits the diagrammatic sketch of reaction pathways. Fig. 5d illustrates the i-t curve of 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY under N2 saturation (0.1 M Na2SO4). The WOR for electron transfer number (n) of Ni-GDY was calculated from Fig. 5d to be 3.77, indicating the occurrence of WOR at the disk electrode for 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY. The diagrammatic sketch of reaction pathways is exhibited in Fig. 5f. Accordingly, the H2O2 generation reaction catalyzed by 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY proceeds through a cascaded two-four electron coupling process, leading to the oxidization of H2O to O2 and reduction of O2 into H2O2.

The transient potential scanning (TPS) test was conducted to analyze the kinetics of charge distribution during the reaction. The TPS test employed a self-designed device capable of recording charging currents and corresponding voltages at sub-millisecond scales. Essentially, this test system functions as a capacitor discharging the working electrode at adjustable positive or negative voltages, with a saturated calomel electrode serving as the reference electrode [45]. In the current attenuation curve, resembling the discharging behavior of an RC circuit, the charging current gradually decreases exponentially after capacitor discharge until the system reaches charge equilibrium. Typically, the TPS test was conducted in N2/O2-saturated 0.1 M Na2SO4 solution under both dark and illuminated conditions. The system utilized −0.1 V as the test voltage chosen based on the CV curve to ensure that they do not trigger the redox reaction of the catalysts. Initially, the charge consumption time (τ) under − 1 V was examined, which depends on capacitance and resistance (τ = RC). The adsorption of O2 on the material surface increases the dielectric medium within the capacitor, resulting in increased capacitance and prolonged decay time. Under dark conditions, τ of 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY in N2-saturated solution was measured at 0.012 ms (Fig. 6 a), shorter than τ (0.045 ms, Fig. 6g) in O2. This suggests that 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY exhibits a high adsorption capacity for O2. Under illumination conditions, τ of 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY and Ni-GDY in O2-saturated solution was measured at 0.019 ms (Fig. 6g) and 0.020 ms (Fig. 6h), higher than that of GDY at 0.013 ms (Fig. 6i). Hence, 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY and Ni-GDY demonstrates superior O2 adsorption capacity compared to GDY.

In the TPS system, the point of intersection between the current curves under illumination and dark conditions indicates the photocatalyst's flat band potential, as the photogenerated current approaches zero [46]. The intersection points of TPS current curves of 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY, Ni-GDY and GDY under dark and illumination conditions and the respective voltage curves are illustrated in Fig. 6d-f (N2-saturated solution) in Fig. 6j-l (O2-saturated solution). Typically, for n-type catalyst materials, the conduction band of the photocatalyst resides approximately 0.1 V below the flat band potential [34]. The conduction bands of 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY, Ni-GDY and GDY calculated by TPS are exhibited in Fig. 6m (vs. RHE). The ECB bond of Ni-GDY was calculated as −0.299 V (vs. RHE) in N2 atmosphere, which consistents with previous findings (−0.295 V vs. RHE). The ECB of 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY, Ni-GDY and GDY are −0.192, −0.168 and −0.353 V (vs. RHE) under O2 condition (working condition). The ECB of 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY and Ni-GDY are slightly under O2 conditions compared to N2 conditions, however, this shift does not alter the overall reaction pathway [47].

The current curves from the TPS test in both N2/O2-saturated 0.1 M Na2SO4 solutions were examined, representing the charges generated or consumed at the catalyst interface. As the integration time approaches 0, the integration of the i–t curve represents the transient current value, indicating the peak separation of electron-hole pairs on the catalyst during illumination. With an initial voltage of −1 V, the current of GDY rises by 187 % after illumination in N2, and by 138 % in O2 (Fig. 6c and Fig. 6f). The current of Ni-GDY increases by 91 % after illumination in N2, and by 118 % in O2 (Fig. 6b and e). Meanwhile, the current of 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY increases by 74 % after illumination in N2, and by 183 % in O2. This implies that the adsorption of O2 facilitates the efficient separation and migration of photoinduced charge carriers within 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY.

Transient photovoltage (TPV) tests were employed to investigate the interfacial charge transfer kinetics of the catalysts. Fig. 7 a and b present the TPV results for CDs and GDY, during the test, the system was initially filled with N2, and then different concentrations of oxygen (ranging from 0 % to 10 %) were introduced for testing. The photocurrent intensity of CDs shows no significant difference between the 100 % N2 and 10 % O2/N2 atmospheres, indicating that O2 has minimal influence on CDs. The photocurrent intensity of GDY shows slight difference between the 0 % O2 and 10 % O2 atmospheres, indicating that O2 has limited influence on GDY. Fig. 7c and d present the TPV results for Ni-GDY and 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY. The overall current intensity of CDs, GDY, Ni-GDY and 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY in different O2/N2 atmosphere are exhibited in Fig. 7e. Compared to GDY, the photocurrent intensity of Ni-GDY exhibits a pronounced dependence on variations in oxygen concentration and the photocurrent response of the 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY composite to oxygen is even more significant. Notably, the photocurrent intensity of Ni-GDY is highly sensitive to changes in O2 concentration, and the presence of CDs amplifies this effect. This suggests that oxygen reduction occurs at the surface of Ni-GDY, highlighting the significantly enhanced reactivity of Ni-GDY in an oxygen-rich environment, with CDs further promoting photo-induced reactivity.

Fig. 8 a presents the TPV curves for CDs, GDY, Ni-GDY, and 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY. The TPV tests were conducted to investigate the interfacial charge transfer kinetics of the catalyst systems. The electron decay constant (τ) for these materials is shown in Fig. 8b. The τ value for 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY (0.295 ms) is higher than that for Ni-GDY (0.263 ms), indicating that the incorporation of CDs into Ni-GDY slows down the electron-hole recombination process. After amplification and area integration of the original TPV data, the maximum charge transfer rate (Tmax) was calculated, as shown in Fig. 8c. CDs exhibit the highest Tmax (0.078 ms), followed by 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY (0.056 ms), GDY (0.046 ms), and Ni-GDY (0.042 ms), suggesting that Ni-GDY facilitates a faster charge separation process. The maximum charge extraction (A) was calculated in Fig. 8d to evaluate the photogenerated charge on the catalyst surface. The 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY composite shows the highest A value (0.225), compared to CDs (0.043), GDY (0.100), and Ni-GDY (0.114), indicating that it accumulates the largest amount of photogenerated charge on the surface. To evaluate the overall photocatalytic reactivity, the surface effective electron numbers (ne = A*τ/Tmax) for CDs, GDY, Ni-GDY, and 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY were calculated and presented in Table S3. It is evident that the ne value for 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY (ne = 1.185) is significantly higher than that for Ni-GDY (ne = 0.714) and the original GDY (ne = 0.602), suggesting that 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY exhibits superior photocatalytic activity compared to both Ni-GDY and GDY. In general, Ni-GDY demonstrates better photocatalytic performance than the original GDY, and the incorporation of CDs further enhances the photocatalytic capability by reducing the electron-hole recombination rate and increasing the accumulation of photogenerated charges on the surface.

Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations were performed to gain a deeper understanding of the photocatalytic reaction occurring on the 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY surface. Initially, we constructed the structure of 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY surface at Fig. S22. The electron transfer process was visualized through charge density difference maps (Fig. 9 a), where yellow regions indicate electron accumulation, while blue regions represent electron depletion. This color mapping helps to identify regions of electron accumulation or loss and to determine the direction of electron transfer. The differential charge density analysis reveals that electrons are transferred from the surrounding C and H atoms to CDs. Subsequently, we examined the adsorption configurations of O2 at three distinct sites. Fig. 9b illustrates the adsorption configuration of the O2-CDs structure, while Fig. 9c and d depict the O2-Ni and O2-CH≡CH structures, respectively. The adsorption energy (Eads) of O2 was found to be higher on the carbon site and the Ni site, with values of −1.39 eV and −1.38 eV, respectively. Detailed adsorption energy data are presented in Table S4. In general, the stability ranking of the adsorption sites is as follows: CDs site > Ni site > CH≡CH site. The DFT calculations proves that CDs have the ability of electron adsorption and strong O2 adsorption ability. Thus, we propose a potential mechanism for photocatalytic H2O2 production using 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY, as follows. Briefly, photogenerated electrons and holes produced H2O2 within the 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY catalyst through ORR and WOR process. The electrons participate in the reduction of O2 adsorbed on the CDs, leading to the production of H2O2. The holes drive the WOR, generating O2. The O2 produced in the WOR subsequently participates in the ORR, acting as a reactant and ultimately leading to the generation of H2O2.

In summary, we have developed a CDs modified nickel-metalated graphdiyne (10 % CDs/Ni-GDY) catalyst for efficient photocatalytic H2O2 production. This catalyst achieves a remarkable H2O2 production rate of 1755.18 μmol·h−1·g−1 in pure water through simultaneous two-electron ORR and four-electron WOR pathways. Comprehensive investigations using TPV and TPS tests on the dynamics of excited charges reveal a potential reaction mechanism for 10 % CDs/Ni-GDY, offering new insights for future research. These findings can be attributed to the superior O2 adsorption capacity and electron-affinity properties of CDs, as well as the enhanced photocatalytic activity of Ni.
